I have got to read the book. I find stories about oil fascinating. I have never heard of this story.  I certainly need to watch the movie. 

Usually, when you can't grow crops on land, it means there is oil underneath.

There is another good movie about oil. The actual book is called OIL!  it's a true story but different, but it does show how certain people got greedy during the oil rush. The movie is called There Will Be Blood. The actual author of the book has a very interesting background. You might want to take a look at a book called The Jungle, the unedited version of how the workforce was without labor laws in the USA. 

There is also an interesting story about a man from Pennsylvania who started the oil industry ideas that came up with the Derrick idea.

textDisplay: Sarah Rector was an African American girl, so why is the lead not African American? I'm not sure what this pattern is about of not having African Americans actors/actresses play the lead in stories about our people. We fought for those opportunities to tell our story, but we seem to be overlooked when these opportunities come along. I want to see African Americans (descendants of the enslaved Africans) reap the benefits of what our ancestors fought for. Yes, we know African American history is rich with giants (Martin Luther King, Harriet Tubman, Whitney Houston, Fred Hampton etc.), so stop rifling through the stories of our ancestors, and ignoring African American descendants.

textDisplay:
The parcel allotted to Sarah Rector was located in Glenpool, 60 miles (97 km) from where she and her family lived. *Its infertile soil was considered unsuitable for farming, with better land being reserved for white settlers and members of the tribe*

*The law at the time required full-blooded Indians, black adults, and children who were citizens of Indian Territory with significant property and money to be assigned "well-respected" white guardians* [15] Thus, as soon as Sarah began to receive this windfall, there was pressure to change her guardianship from her parents to a local white resident and family acquaintance named T.J. (or J.T.) Porter.

 As news of Rector's wealth spread worldwide, she received requests for loans, money gifts, and marriage proposals, though she was only 11 years old.[14] *Due to her wealth, in 1913, the Oklahoma Legislature made an effort to have her declared an honorary white, allowing her the benefits of elevated social standing, such as riding in a first-class car on the trains*

James C. Waters Jr., sent a memo to Du Bois regarding her situation. Waters had been corresponding with the Bureau of Indian Affairs and the United States Children's Bureau over concerns regarding the mismanagement of Rector's estate.  *He wrote of her white financial guardian: Is it not possible to have her cared for in a decent manner and by people of her own race, instead of by a member of a race which would deny her and her kind the treatment accorded a good yard dog*

Yet we see a trailer of a young Sarah walking around bright eyed and innocent just asking strange white men to help her with her land and oil....🤣🤣🤣🤣🤣  standard hollywood blacks are noble sufferers who need the help of good whites to fights of the bad apples in their race.🙄🙄🙄🙄 talk about WOKE nonsense..

textDisplay: What a wonderful Truth about Black History in America being shared with the rest of our World! People be soo quick to downplay our joy of seeing ourselves positively by saying, "Oh well there are OTHER races that got left out of the history books!" This not about you right now🙄.

textDisplay: Sarah's Oil" tells the true story of Sarah Rector, an African American girl who became one of the nation's first female millionaires at the age of 11 after oil was discovered on her Oklahoma land allotment. Born in 1902 in Indian Territory, Sarah's family, descendants of Creek Indians, were allotted land, some of which was later found to be rich in oil. Despite facing poverty and skepticism about the value of her land, Sarah believed in its potential and, with the help of her family and some Texas wildcatters, successfully navigated the challenges of oil speculation and became incredibly wealthy.

textDisplay:
Sarah Rector (1902-1967) was an American oil magnate who became one of the wealthiest Black women in the United States as a child. Her story is a remarkable account of how an unexpected discovery of oil transformed her life from poverty to immense wealth, while also highlighting the systemic racism and exploitation of the early 20th century.
Early Life and The Discovery of Wealth
 * Birth and Heritage: Sarah Rector was born in 1902 in the Indian Territory, near the all-Black town of Taft (which is now part of Oklahoma). Her parents were descendants of Black people who had been enslaved by the Muscogee Creek Nation and were granted land as part of the Dawes Allotment Act of 1887 and the Treaty of 1866.
 * Land Allotment: As a child, Sarah was allotted 160 acres of land. The land was considered poor and not suitable for farming, and her family struggled to pay the annual property taxes of $30 on it. To cover the costs, her father leased the land to the Standard Oil Company in 1911.
 * The Oil Gusher: In 1913, an independent oil driller struck a massive oil gusher on her property. The well produced over 2,500 barrels of oil per day, and overnight, Sarah became extraordinarily wealthy. She was earning over $300 a day in royalties, a staggering amount at the time. This led to her being dubbed "the richest colored girl in the world."
The Challenges and Public Scrutiny
 * Guardianship: Because she was a minor with such significant assets, Oklahoma law required that a white guardian be appointed to manage her estate. This practice, along with her newfound wealth, drew immense national attention and scrutiny.
 * Misinformation and Advocacy: The media sensationalized her story, and rumors began to spread about her being uneducated, mistreated, and living in poverty despite her fortune. These false reports prompted an investigation by the NAACP and notable figures like W.E.B. Du Bois, who were concerned about the exploitation of Black children's wealth. The investigation ultimately found the rumors to be false, and Sarah was later enrolled at the Tuskegee Institute.
Later Life and Legacy
 * A Millionaire by 18: By the time she turned 18, Sarah Rector was a millionaire, with a diverse portfolio that included stocks, bonds, businesses, and more real estate. She moved to Kansas City, Missouri, where she and her family lived a comfortable life.
 * Lifestyle: She enjoyed her wealth, living in a large home, owning fine clothes and cars, and hosting lavish parties that were attended by celebrities like Duke Ellington and Count Basie.
 * The Great Depression and Death: Sarah Rector lost a significant portion of her fortune during the Great Depression. She died in 1967 at the age of 65, still owning some oil wells and real estate.
Sarah Rector's life is a powerful and complex story that stands as a symbol of unexpected fortune and the racial injustices prevalent in early 20th-century America. Her case helped to shed light on the systemic issues of guardianship and wealth management for Black individuals at the time.

textDisplay: I first wrote about Sarah Rector's story when I was in graduate school working on my dissertation.  I consulted with Tonya Bolden who later wrote a book on her.  And I've interviewed relatives of Rector. This trailer is full of whitewashing.  A child, by law, could not enter into contracts because they were considered "incompetent."  Neither Sarah nor her parents were consulted.  The Muskogee County court system appointed her a white guardian, like other Creek Freedman minors and Indian children who were granted land through the Dawes Allotment Act.  She never shook hands with grown white men and negotiated deals over her land.  Her white guardian tried to swindle her.  To the point where W.E.B. DuBois had to intervene in her case, establishing the Children's Department under the NAACP to safequard her and other wealthy Black minors.  Once they got the court to intervene to stop the chicanery, she was sent to a boarding school at Tuskegee.  By the time she was of age, that same white man still tried to fight her over her estate.  She appeared in court again and won.  Those white men in Oklahoma were unscrupulous and HATED that so many Freedman minors and Indian children were allotted lands, especially lands with oil and minerals.  They even murdered Black children to get their lands.  And they used the county courts to appoint guardians who ended up stealing generational wealth from these kids and their families.  Ugh.

Neither Sarah nor her parents were consulted.  The Muskogee County court system appointed her a white guardian, like other Creek Freedman minors and Indian children who were granted land through the Dawes Allotment Act.  She never shook hands with grown white men and negotiated deals over her land.  Her white guardian tried to swindle her.  To the point where W.E.B. DuBois had to intervene in her case, establishing the Children's Department under the NAACP to safequard her and other wealthy Black minors.  Once they got the court to intervene to stop the chicanery, she was sent to a boarding school at Tuskegee.  By the time she was of age, that same white man still tried to fight her over her estate.  She appeared in court again and won.  Those white men in Oklahoma were unscrupulous and HATED that so many Freedman minors and Indian children were allotted lands, especially lands with oil and minerals.  They even murdered Black children to get their lands.  And they used the county courts to appoint guardians who ended up stealing generational wealth from these kids and their families.  Ugh.

I know members of Sarah's family. They are incredibly excited someone is doing this story the right way. Sarah's family still lives in the Kansas City area and have been trying to correct misconceptions and false information for years.

I was happy to hear the filmmakers reached out to consult to family about this movie.
textOriginal:
I know members of Sarah's family. They are incredibly excited someone is doing this story the right way. Sarah's family still lives in the Kansas City area and have been trying to correct misconceptions and false information for years.

I was happy to hear the filmmakers reached out to consult to family about this movie.

textDisplay: What misconception?! Please explain further.
textOriginal: What misconception?! Please explain further.

textDisplay:
@Superzealite for one, the most famous picture of Sarah Rector as a girl isn't even her and looks nothing like her as a child. They've been trying to get that switched for years.

A lot of the story that is out there is partially incorrect or has assumptions about her life pieced together from different news articles and many historians that study her never reach out to the family to get the facts. Timelines are wrong and much of her later life isn't as well known.

From what I remember, her childhood story is more accurate because there's a lot more legal paperwork to refer to regarding her wealth, schooling, and the white financial guardian she was given. 

There's one group in Oklahoma that was working on stuff and they had added an entire generation in the family tree that didn't exist and wondered why they couldn't find records. They also didn't realize how much of Sarah's family was living in KC. They had her nieces listed as "great-nieces." They were shocked when Sarah's nieces showed up and could fill in so many parts of the story because they heard it directly from "Aunt Sarah" as children.

From what I remember, her childhood story is more accurate because there's a lot more legal paperwork to refer to regarding her wealth, schooling, and the white financial guardian she was given. 

There's one group in Oklahoma that was working on stuff and they had added an entire generation in the family tree that didn't exist and wondered why they couldn't find records. They also didn't realize how much of Sarah's family was living in KC. They had her nieces listed as "great-nieces." They were shocked when Sarah's nieces showed up and could fill in so many parts of the story because they heard it directly from "Aunt Sarah" as children.

textDisplay: Hate how the school systems decide to brush over extra extraordinary stories of people in the past but rather focus intently on the horrors of systemic trauma (which to be fair, I get it to a certain extent), almost every year I’m learning about someone new who’s story shapes the way things are how there today, regardless of their race.

textDisplay: To be honest, I don't think it's that. If you think of history as a narrative or continuum, this is stand alone and school just tries to give you the narrative and SHOULD encourage you to find these stories on your own time. But I hear enough people complain about how boring history is...I don't think that's succeeding. I was lucky.
textOriginal: To be honest, I don't think it's that. If you think of history as a narrative or continuum, this is stand alone and school just tries to give you the narrative and SHOULD encourage you to find these stories on your own time. But I hear enough people complain about how boring history is...I don't think that's succeeding. I was lucky.
